summary: Joel Spolsky revisits his 'architecture astronaut' concept to criticize Microsoft's launch of Live Mesh, a cloud synchronization platform he sees as yet another repackaging of the same failed idea. He traces a lineage from Microsoft's Hailstorm (2001) through Groove to Live Mesh, arguing that Ray Ozzie keeps rebuilding the same synchronization platform that nobody asked for. Spolsky contends that file synchronization is simply not a killer application, despite architects repeatedly treating it as the foundation for the next great platform. He broadens his criticism to both Microsoft and Google for hoarding top CS talent to work on grandiose but useless architecture projects, driving up salaries while producing nothing customers actually want.
key_points: Microsoft Live Mesh is essentially the same cloud synchronization concept as Hailstorm and Groove, repackaged yet again after repeated market failures
Architecture astronauts build elaborate platforms and frameworks for problems that aren't actually problems, mistaking interesting engineering challenges for customer needs
File synchronization has been attempted dozens of times since 1999 and has never become a killer application despite seeming like it should be
Microsoft and Google are absorbing too many talented programmers into pointless projects, inflating CS salaries while producing nothing of real value
Real user-loved products like Twitter, Flickr, and Delicious succeed by solving actual wants, not by building grand architectural visions
money_quotes: That's one sure tip-off to the fact that you're being assaulted by an Architecture Astronaut: the incredible amount of bombast; the heroic, utopian grandiloquence; the boastfulness; the complete lack of reality.
The hallmark of an architecture astronaut is that they don't solve an actual problem… they solve something that appears to be the template of a lot of problems.
Nobody cared then and nobody cares now, because synchronizing files is just not a killer application.
Ray Ozzie just can't stop rewriting this damn app, again and again and again, and taking 5-7 years each time.
The fact that customers never asked for this feature and none of the earlier versions really took off as huge platforms doesn't stop him.

This so called synchronization problem is just not an actual problem, it's a fun programming exercise that you're doing because it's just hard enough to be interesting but not so hard that you can't figure it out.
Here it is Wednesday and you've rebuilt the whole goddamn TV dinner industry from the ground up and you're giving me 1955 salisbury steak that I just DON'T WANT.
key_insight: Architecture astronauts repeatedly build elaborate platforms for problems nobody has, mistaking intellectually interesting engineering challenges for genuine customer needs.
